batch_products.browser_capture: Amazon Deals document plus data.amazon.com product-v2 batch Fetch
batch_products.captured_contract: public Deals HTML supplies encrypted-slate-token and csrfToken; both are replayed in request headers and never exposed to the caller
batch_products.comparison: exact title, price, list price, discount, and addability match against the existing two-product HTML comparison for the returned fields

deals.comparison: exact ASIN, order, title, and price match for all five results

best_sellers.comparison: exact rank, ASIN, title, and price match for all five results

product.comparison: exact title, price, rating, and availability match
product.title: Apple EarPods Headphones with USB-C Plug, Wired Ear Buds with Built-in Remote to Control Music, Phone Calls, and Volume

anonymous_cart.comparison: same item, quantity, unit price, and zero-item state after removal; both routes normalize the empty subtotal to $0.00

five_run_benchmark.method: Warm browser session and warm direct process; direct response cache disabled; browser waits until compared result fields are present. Deals browser timing includes opening Deals, filling the search form, submitting, and observing results.
five_run_benchmark.interpretation: Direct replay was 2.18x faster for the multi-step Deals search, but about 10 percent slower for the one-page product and Best Sellers reads. The network benchmark excludes model reasoning and token cost; direct replay still needs one compact structured call while browser automation needs navigation, page inspection, interaction, and extraction.

limitations: The browser and direct anonymous carts used separate cookie jars, so semantic state was compared rather than shared-session identity.
The benchmark measures website and automation wall time, not model token usage; this runtime does not expose an exact per-tool token counter.
No authenticated Amazon operation was tested because no account was available.
